questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a client taking warfarin shows bleeding risk and a rising INR?
questions.scenario: A client taking warfarin for atrial fibrillation reports black stools and bleeding gums.
questions.additionalInfo: Today's INR is markedly above therapeutic range and the next dose is due soon.

options.text: Hold the dose, assess bleeding severity, and escalate reversal planning per protocol
Give the next dose on time because stopping anticoagulation is always more dangerous
Encourage extra green vegetables right now instead of notifying the provider
Tell the client mild bleeding is expected and to call back next week if it worsens

questions.rationale: Bleeding symptoms with a supratherapeutic INR require immediate hold-and-escalate thinking, not routine dosing. The nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment.

distractorRationales.b: "Give the next dose on time because stopping anticoagulation is always more dangerous" misses the bedside risk because the nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward escalation instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "Today's INR is markedly above therapeutic range and the next dose is due soon" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Tell the client mild bleeding is expected and to call back next week if it worsens" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "The nurse should assess the extent of bleeding and follow provider/protocol guidance for reversal or dose adjustment" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Bleeding plus high INR means pause the anticoagulant and escalate.
questions.speedCue: Black stools plus high INR means hold before you harm.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a hyperthyroid client suddenly deteriorates?
questions.scenario: A client with untreated hyperthyroidism becomes agitated, febrile, tachycardic, and diaphoretic after surgery.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is now vomiting and appears increasingly confused.

options.text: Treat this as thyroid storm and escalate rapid supportive and medication-focused care
Reassure the client that postoperative anxiety explains all of the findings
Delay action until the temperature trend is repeated in several hours
Encourage ambulation to reduce the heart rate and agitation

questions.rationale: Hyperthermia, severe tachycardia, agitation, and GI symptoms in a hyperthyroid client point to thyroid storm. The nurse should escalate immediately because this is a decompensation pattern, not routine anxiety or pain alone.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature teaching instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "The client is now vomiting and appears increasingly confused" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Delay action until the temperature trend is repeated in several hours" misses the bedside risk because hyperthermia, severe tachycardia, agitation points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: Because the nurse should escalate immediately because this is a decompensation pattern, not routine anxiety or pain alone suggests medication toxicity, "Encourage ambulation to reduce the heart rate and agitation" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: Fever plus tachycardia plus agitation in hyperthyroidism is storm until proved otherwise.
questions.speedCue: Hot, fast, agitated, hyperthyroid after stress means storm thinking now.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when this client becomes increasingly paranoid and agitated?
questions.scenario: A client with schizophrenia is pacing, shouting that staff members are trying to poison him, and clenching his fists.
questions.additionalInfo: The unit is noisy, and several other clients have gathered nearby to watch the scene.

options.text: This is a safety and de-escalation situation that calls for a calm, low-stimulation approach and removal of excess triggers
The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false
The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction
The nurse should leave immediately without setting limits or assessing risk

questions.rationale: Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation, and calm limit setting rather than confrontation. Arguing about the delusion or adding more external stimulation increases the risk that the client's distress escalates into violence.

distractorRationales.b: "The nurse should argue with the delusion to prove the belief is false" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: "The nurse should bring more clients close by to normalize the interaction" misses the bedside risk because acute psychosis with rising agitation should be answered with safety, lower stimulation points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: In psychosis, the nurse leads with safety and calm structure, not debate.
questions.speedCue: Paranoia plus clenched fists means lower the room before you lower the delusion.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move when family demands conflict with a documented client directive?
questions.scenario: A client with metastatic cancer has a signed advance directive declining intubation and CPR.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is now unresponsive, and two adult children insist the nurse should 'do everything' while the provider is en route.

options.text: Honor the documented advance directive while escalating the conflict through the provider and ethics chain
Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes
Delay all action until the family agrees on a plan
Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else

questions.rationale: A valid advance directive reflects the client's expressed wishes and should guide care when the client can no longer speak. The nurse should follow that documented plan while escalating the family conflict through the proper chain rather than treating family pressure as a replacement for the client's autonomy.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Ignore the directive because family requests automatically override prior written wishes" moves care toward waiting for a callback before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: "Delay all action until the family agrees on a plan" misses the bedside risk because advance directive conflict family safety points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "Ask the family to sign a new code-status form at the bedside before doing anything else" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "advance directive conflict family safety" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: When family demands conflict with a valid directive, the client's documented wishes stay at the center of care.
questions.speedCue: The shortcut is autonomy first: a signed directive is the client's voice when the family is distressed.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a newborn feed is painful and ineffective?
questions.scenario: A postpartum client says breastfeeding is sharply painful and the newborn falls asleep after a few shallow sucks.
questions.additionalInfo: The infant has had fewer wet diapers since discharge teaching began earlier that morning.

options.text: Assess latch and positioning, then support a deeper latch with feeding observation
Tell the parent to limit feeds because nipple pain means the baby is feeding too often
Switch to water between feeds until the nipples heal
Recommend stopping breastfeeding for the rest of the day without reassessment

questions.rationale: Pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness. The nurse should reassess positioning and feeding effectiveness before recommending delays or supplements that do not fix the cause.

distractorRationales.b: "Tell the parent to limit feeds because nipple pain means the baby is feeding too often" misses the bedside risk because pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness points to pediatric or newborn instability. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.c: Because pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness suggests pediatric or newborn instability, "Switch to water between feeds until the nipples heal" would not correct the active problem. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.d: "Recommend stopping breastfeeding for the rest of the day without reassessment" misses the bedside risk because pain with shallow ineffective sucking points to latch and transfer problems, not simply normal soreness points to pediatric or newborn instability. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.

questions.takeaway: Pain plus poor output means reassess latch, not just reassure.
questions.speedCue: Painful shallow sucks and fewer wets point to latch failure first.

visualRationale.title: breastfeeding_latch_assessment_intervention
visualRationale.caption: Pain plus poor output means reassess latch, not just reassure.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pain plus poor output means reassess latch, not just reassure.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when intimate-partner violence is suspected during a clinic visit?
questions.scenario: A client arrives with bruising in different stages of healing and glances at the partner before answering questions.
questions.additionalInfo: The partner insists on staying in the room and answers most questions for the client.

options.text: Create privacy, assess immediate safety, and offer resources without forcing disclosure
Confront the partner in the room and demand an explanation for the bruises
Tell the client to leave the relationship immediately before any other discussion
Document the bruises only if the client is willing to file a police report during the visit

questions.rationale: Suspected intimate-partner violence requires privacy, safety assessment, supportive documentation, and resource connection. The nurse should not escalate danger by confronting the partner in front of the client or forcing one specific decision.

distractorRationales.b: Because suspected intimate-partner violence requires privacy, safety assessment, supportive documentation suggests clinical safety risk, "Confront the partner in the room and demand an explanation for the bruises" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Tell the client to leave the relationship immediately before any other discussion" misses the bedside risk because suspected intimate-partner violence requires privacy, safety assessment, supportive documentation points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Safety planning starts with privacy and support, not confrontation" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Safety planning starts with privacy and support, not confrontation.
questions.speedCue: If the partner answers for the client, privacy is the next nursing move.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which conclusion should guide the nurse's next move when a bleeding client refuses blood products for religious reasons?
questions.scenario: A postoperative client who is alert and oriented has a hemoglobin drop, tachycardia, and increasing drainage at the incision.
questions.additionalInfo: The client states, 'I do not accept blood transfusions,' while the spouse begs the staff to override that choice.

options.text: Honor the client's refusal while escalating the bleeding emergency through the provider and accepted alternatives
Prepare blood immediately because the spouse has requested life-saving treatment
Delay provider notification until the spouse and client agree on one plan
Tell the spouse to sign the consent because the client is making an unsafe decision

questions.rationale: An informed competent refusal remains the client's decision even during a bleeding emergency. The nurse should escalate the instability and coordinate accepted alternatives rather than allowing family pressure to replace the client's autonomy.

distractorRationales.b: Because a postoperative client who is alert and oriented has a hemoglobin drop, tachycardia suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Prepare blood immediately because the spouse has requested life-saving treatment" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: Because an informed competent refusal remains the client's decision even during a bleeding emergency suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Delay provider notification until the spouse and client agree on one plan" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: "Tell the spouse to sign the consent because the client is making an unsafe decision" misses the bedside risk because an informed competent refusal remains the client's decision even during a bleeding emergency points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Competent refusal stays with the client even when the spouse is distressed.
questions.speedCue: Bleeding emergency changes urgency, not who owns the decision.

questions.stem: Which interpretation should guide the nurse's next move after this abnormal gestational-diabetes screen?
questions.scenario: A pregnant client at 28 weeks has an abnormal glucose screening result and says, 'I only feel thirsty because I'm pregnant.'
questions.additionalInfo: The client asks whether anything really needs to change if the baby is moving normally.

options.text: This result needs follow-up teaching and blood-glucose management planning because untreated hyperglycemia raises maternal and fetal risk
The result is expected in pregnancy and does not need any follow-up unless contractions begin
The client should stop all carbohydrates immediately until the next prenatal visit
Fetal movement alone rules out meaningful glucose-related risk

questions.rationale: An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes. The nurse should frame this as ongoing health-promotion and risk-reduction work, not as a benign pregnancy symptom pattern.

distractorRationales.b: Because an abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes suggests unstable glucose physiology, "The result is expected in pregnancy and does not need any follow-up unless contractions begin" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "The client should stop all carbohydrates immediately until the next prenatal visit" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Fetal movement alone rules out meaningful glucose-related risk" misses the bedside risk because an abnormal gestational-diabetes screen needs follow-up and counseling because hyperglycemia affects both maternal and fetal outcomes points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: An abnormal gestational-diabetes screen is a management issue, not a reassurance cue.
questions.speedCue: Normal fetal movement does not cancel a maternal glucose problem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after an adolescent screens positive for depression?
questions.scenario: A 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit.
questions.additionalInfo: The adolescent shrugs and says, 'I'm just tired all the time. It's not a big deal.'

options.text: Treat the screen as meaningful, assess safety directly, and connect the adolescent with follow-up support
Reassure the adolescent that screening tools often overreact and no follow-up is needed
Skip all direct questions about self-harm because they may plant the idea
Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms

questions.rationale: A positive depression screen is a prompt for direct safety assessment and follow-up support, not a reassurance cue. The nurse should not avoid suicide questions or delay referral simply because the adolescent is minimizing symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Reassure the adolescent that screening tools often overreact and no follow-up is needed" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: "Skip all direct questions about self-harm because they may plant the idea" misses the bedside risk because a 16-year-old scores in the moderate range on a depression screening tool during a school-based clinic visit points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: Because a positive depression screen is a prompt for direct safety assessment and follow-up support, not a reassurance cue suggests clinical safety risk, "Wait until the next annual visit to revisit mood symptoms" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Screening only helps when a positive result leads to direct safety assessment and follow-up.
questions.speedCue: A positive screen means ask directly, not later.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority before this client receives IV contrast?
questions.scenario: A client with chronic kidney disease is scheduled for a contrast-enhanced CT study.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse notes elevated creatinine and asks which prevention frame should guide care before transport.

options.text: Identify renal vulnerability, protect hydration and kidney-safe preparation, and watch post-contrast renal trends
Treat the study as risk-free because contrast only affects clients on dialysis
Delay hydration because IV fluids can interfere with the scanner
Ignore baseline creatinine as long as the client is not reporting pain

questions.rationale: Contrast prevention starts with recognizing renal vulnerability and supporting kidney-protective preparation, especially hydration and medication review when ordered. The nurse should not treat contrast risk as absent just because the client is stable at the bedside.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Treat the study as risk-free because contrast only affects clients on dialysis" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before renal protection.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of renal protection. The stem cue "A client with chronic kidney disease is scheduled for a contrast-enhanced CT study" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Ignore baseline creatinine as long as the client is not reporting pain" misses the bedside risk because a client with chronic kidney disease is scheduled for a contrast-enhanced CT study points to renal injury. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.

questions.takeaway: Stable appearance does not erase contrast risk when the kidneys are already vulnerable.
questions.speedCue: High creatinine before contrast means prevention mode, not routine transport mode.

visualRationale.title: contrast_induced_nephropathy_prevention
visualRationale.caption: Stable appearance does not erase contrast risk when the kidneys are already vulnerable.
visualRationale.conclusion: Stable appearance does not erase contrast risk when the kidneys are already vulnerable.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority after recognizing that the wrong dose of insulin was given?
questions.scenario: The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is awake but now diaphoretic and shaky, and the blood glucose result is pending.

options.text: Treat the client as the priority, assess and intervene for hypoglycemia, notify the provider, then complete the reporting chain
Complete the incident report before assessing the client so the timeline is accurate
Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm
Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed

questions.rationale: After a medication error, the unstable client comes before paperwork. The nurse should assess and treat the potential hypoglycemia first, notify the provider, and then complete the required reporting process rather than allowing documentation to delay urgent care.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "The nurse discovers that 10 units of rapid-acting insulin were administered instead of the prescribed 4 units" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Wait for symptoms to worsen so the provider has clear evidence of harm" moves care toward routine observation before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.d: "Ask the next nurse to decide whether the error needs to be disclosed" misses the bedside risk because the blood glucose result is pending points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Reporting matters, but the harmed or unstable client always comes first.
questions.speedCue: Error chain shortcut: patient first, provider second, paperwork third.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains the nurse's priority for refractory dyspnea in a palliative-care client?
questions.scenario: A client with end-stage pulmonary disease reports air hunger despite oxygen and appears frightened.
questions.additionalInfo: The family asks whether increasing comfort medications means the team is giving up.

options.text: Treat dyspnea as a symptom crisis, use prescribed comfort measures, and coach the family on the goal of relief
Avoid symptom medication because sedation always means care is no longer active
Stop all oxygen and repositioning because they no longer matter in palliative care
Delay symptom relief until the family agrees on every part of the plan

questions.rationale: Palliative care actively treats dyspnea and distress, and comfort-focused medication can be appropriate when prescribed. The nurse should frame the goal as relief of suffering, not abandonment of care.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Treat dyspnea as a symptom crisis, use prescribed comfort measures, and coach the family on the" fits the safer response to palliative care actively treats dyspnea and distress.
distractorRationales.c: "Stop all oxygen and repositioning because they no longer matter in palliative care" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "A client with end-stage pulmonary disease reports air hunger despite oxygen and appears frightened" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: Because a client with end-stage pulmonary disease reports air hunger despite oxygen and appears frightened suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Delay symptom relief until the family agrees on every part of the plan" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Comfort care still treats symptoms aggressively when the goal is relief.
questions.speedCue: Air hunger in palliative care is a treat-now symptom problem, not a wait-for-consensus problem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this client's rapid deterioration?
questions.scenario: A client with chronic adrenal insufficiency has been vomiting for 24 hours and now looks weak and confused.
questions.additionalInfo: Blood pressure is 82/46 mm Hg, sodium is 124 mEq/L, potassium is 5.8 mEq/L, and the morning steroid dose was missed.

options.text: Adrenal crisis requiring immediate steroid replacement, fluid support, and shock-focused escalation
Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids
Expected fatigue from chronic illness without emergency risk
A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up

questions.rationale: Vomiting, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and missed steroids create an adrenal-crisis map. The nurse should answer from endocrine shock rescue, not from comfort care or delayed reassessment.

distractorRationales.b: "Simple dehydration that only needs oral fluids" misses the bedside risk because blood pressure is 82/46 mm Hg, sodium is 124 mEq/L, potassium is 5 points to renal injury. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.c: "Expected fatigue from chronic illness without emergency risk" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "Blood pressure is 82/46 mm Hg, sodium is 124 mEq/L, potassium is 5" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: "A benign stomach virus that can wait for clinic follow-up" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Blood pressure is 82/46 mm Hg, sodium is 124 mEq/L, potassium is 5" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: The lab pattern matters most when it arrives with hypotension and missed steroid coverage.
questions.speedCue: Hypotension plus low sodium plus high potassium should feel like adrenal crisis fast.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which clinical map should guide the nurse's next move after this transfusion starts?
questions.scenario: Fifteen minutes after packed red blood cells begin, a client reports chills, low-back pain, and chest tightness.
questions.additionalInfo: The transfusion is still running and the nurse is choosing between documenting, slowing, or stopping the blood.

options.text: Acute transfusion reaction requiring the blood to be stopped and the reaction protocol activated
Expected warming discomfort that only needs reassurance
Anxiety from the hospital setting without transfusion relevance
A minor symptom set that should be charted while the blood continues

questions.rationale: Chills, low-back pain, and chest tightness soon after transfusion create a reaction map, not a comfort issue. The nurse should answer from stop-the-blood-first logic.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "The transfusion is still running and the nurse is choosing between documenting, slowing, or stopping the blood" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Anxiety from the hospital setting without transfusion relevance" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "The transfusion is still running and the nurse is choosing between documenting, slowing, or stopping the blood" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: The first safe move is driven by timing plus symptom cluster, not by waiting for more proof.
questions.speedCue: New reaction symptoms soon after blood starts means stop the blood first.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which decision map best explains this change after fracture care?
questions.scenario: A client with a new lower-leg cast reports escalating pain that is not relieved by opioids.
questions.additionalInfo: The toes are pale, numb, and increasingly painful with passive stretch.

options.text: Compartment syndrome requiring urgent pressure-relief escalation and neurovascular protection
Expected postoperative pain that can wait for the next dose of medication
Routine swelling without perfusion concern
Simple anxiety because the cast feels tight

questions.rationale: Pain out of proportion with neurovascular decline creates a compartment syndrome map. The nurse should answer from perfusion rescue, not analgesia alone.

distractorRationales.b: Because pain out of proportion with neurovascular decline creates a compartment syndrome map suggests neurologic deterioration, "Expected postoperative pain that can wait for the next dose of medication" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.c: "Routine swelling without perfusion concern" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Pain out of proportion with neurovascular decline creates a compartment syndrome map" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: Because pain out of proportion with neurovascular decline creates a compartment syndrome map suggests neurologic deterioration, "Simple anxiety because the cast feels tight" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: The pain pattern matters more than the cast itself.
questions.speedCue: Pain out of proportion plus passive-stretch pain is the shortcut.

questions.stem: Which findings in this febrile client support the nurse's concern for meningococcal sepsis? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A college student arrives with fever, severe headache, malaise, and rapidly worsening illness.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding which additional findings would strengthen concern for a meningococcal emergency rather than a routine viral illness.

options.text: A nonblanching petechial or purpuric rash
Hypotension or rapidly worsening perfusion
Neck stiffness and photophobia
A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure
Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids
Acute confusion or worsening mental status

questions.rationale: Meningococcal sepsis clues cluster around invasive infection and rapid deterioration: nonblanching rash, perfusion decline, meningeal irritation, and mental-status change. The nurse should exclude benign or improving patterns that do not fit invasive sepsis.

distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses infection progression; "A mild itchy rash that fades with pressure" moves care toward reassurance before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.e: Because the fast clue is not fever alone suggests infection progression, "Warm, stable vitals with improving symptoms after fluids" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: The fast clue is not fever alone, but fever plus rash and collapse signs.
questions.speedCue: Nonblanching rash plus neuro change or shock is the shortcut.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which findings make severe symptomatic hyponatremia the best interpretation? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A client taking a thiazide diuretic becomes progressively more confused during admission.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the change is mostly electrolyte collapse or routine fatigue.

options.text: New confusion with headache
Seizure activity after worsening mentation
Serum sodium of 118 mEq/L
Dry hacking cough for 1 week
Normal orientation and steady gait

questions.rationale: The key cluster is a very low sodium level plus acute neurologic deterioration. The nurse should read the lab and the brain symptoms together, because that combination changes the urgency immediately.

distractorRationales.d: "Dry hacking cough for 1 week" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse is deciding whether the change is mostly electrolyte collapse or routine fatigue" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.e: Because the key cluster is a very low sodium level plus acute neurologic deterioration suggests neurologic deterioration, "Normal orientation and steady gait" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: A sodium number matters more when the nervous system is already failing in front of you.
questions.speedCue: Low sodium plus neuro change is the shortcut; don't split the clue cluster apart.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which findings support the nurse's concern that this newborn is becoming hypoglycemic? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario: A newborn of a gestational-diabetic parent is 3 hours old and is becoming harder to feed.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is deciding whether the bedside change fits a glucose problem or a routine transition period.

options.text: Jitteriness with tremulous extremities
Weak or poor feeding effort
Low axillary temperature
Strong lusty cry with vigorous rooting
Pink color and normal spontaneous wakefulness

questions.rationale: Newborn hypoglycemia often shows up as jitteriness, poor feeding, and temperature instability. The nurse should cluster the subtle findings together quickly instead of waiting for a dramatic crash.

distractorRationales.d: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Jitteriness with tremulous extremities plus Weak or poor feeding effort plus Low axillary temperature" fits the safer response to a newborn of a gestational-diabetic parent is 3 hours old and is becoming harder to feed.
distractorRationales.e: "Pink color and normal spontaneous wakefulness" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "A newborn of a gestational-diabetic parent is 3 hours old and is becoming harder to feed" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.
questions.speedCue: Jitteriness plus poor feeding should move glucose near the top immediately.

visualRationale.title: newborn_hypoglycemia_jitteriness
visualRationale.caption: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.
visualRationale.conclusion: In newborns, multiple small instability clues often matter more than one dramatic sign.

questions.stem: A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness, and left shoulder pain. Which findings support the nurse's concern for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy?
questions.scenario: The client looks pale and anxious in triage. Blood pressure is 86/48 mm Hg and heart rate is 128/min.

options.text: Hypotension
Shoulder pain
Rigid abdomen
Light pink spotting only
Tachycardia
Improving pelvic discomfort after rest

questions.rationale: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia, and referred shoulder pain from intraperitoneal bleeding. The nurse should connect these findings to hemorrhagic instability instead of treating them as routine first-trimester discomfort.

distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Hypotension plus Shoulder pain plus Tachycardia" fits the safer response to referred shoulder pain from intraperitoneal bleeding.
distractorRationales.f: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Improving pelvic discomfort after rest" moves care toward routine care before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: When pregnancy, unilateral pain, shoulder pain, and shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast.
questions.speedCue: Pregnancy plus unilateral pain plus shoulder pain plus hypotension is a hemorrhage pattern, not a routine discomfort pattern.

questions.stem: Place the nursing actions in the order that best follows fire safety priorities when smoke is seen near an oxygen source.
questions.scenario: Smoke is seen near an electrical device in a client's room while the client is receiving oxygen.
questions.additionalInfo: The client is awake but weak and needs assistance to leave the room safely.

options.text: Rescue the client from immediate danger
Activate the alarm and call for help
Contain the fire by closing doors and managing the room space
Extinguish only if the fire is small and safe to approach with the correct device

questions.rationale: RACE still governs the first moves: rescue, alarm, contain, then extinguish if safe. Oxygen and electrical fire clues raise urgency, so the nurse removes the client from danger before trying to manage equipment or flames.
questions.takeaway: Rescue comes before equipment cleanup when smoke and oxygen are in the same room.
questions.speedCue: Smoke plus oxygen means RACE immediately, not troubleshooting first.

visualRationale.title: fire_safety_RACE_PASS_oxygen
visualRationale.caption: Rescue comes before equipment cleanup when smoke and oxygen are in the same room.
visualRationale.conclusion: Rescue comes before equipment cleanup when smoke and oxygen are in the same room.

questions.stem: A client says, 'I don't think I can keep doing this.' Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The client has major depressive disorder and was admitted earlier today.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse has privacy, immediate supervision support, and access to the unit's suicide-risk protocol.

options.text: Stay with the client and create immediate safety
Ask direct questions about suicidal thoughts, plan, and means
Notify the provider and activate the unit suicide-safety protocol
Remove dangerous items and increase observation based on risk

questions.rationale: Suicide-risk care starts with staying with the client and asking directly about thoughts, plan, and means. Once the risk is clarified, the nurse escalates through the protocol and removes access to self-harm opportunities rather than minimizing the statement or leaving the client alone.
questions.takeaway: When the client hints at self-harm, stay, ask directly, escalate, and secure the environment.
questions.speedCue: Do not leave and do not soften the question.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports a higher FLACC pain score or a lower FLACC pain score.
questions.scenario: A preverbal child is recovering after surgery and cannot rate pain verbally.
questions.additionalInfo: The nurse is using the FLACC approach to sort behaviors into pain-related or calmer findings.

options.text: Supports higher FLACC score
Supports lower FLACC score

matrixRows.label: Legs drawn up and restless movement
Quiet sleep with neutral facial expression

questions.rationale: The FLACC scale depends on behavioral clues rather than a verbal report. Grimacing, restlessness, and withdrawal behaviors support more pain concern, while relaxed, easily consoled behavior points the nurse toward lower pain intensity.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave pediatric or newborn instability untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "the row-specific answer pattern" fits the safer response to the nurse is using the FLACC approach to sort behaviors into pain-related or calmer findings.
distractorRationales.b: "Supports lower FLACC score" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "while relaxed, easily consoled behavior points the nurse toward lower pain intensity" raises concern for pediatric or newborn instability, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: When the client cannot rate pain, behavior becomes the pain language.
questions.speedCue: Restless, grimacing, hard-to-console behaviors belong in the higher-pain column.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding supports worsening acute kidney injury or a less urgent monitoring picture.
questions.scenario: The nurse is comparing several renal function trends for a client whose kidney status is worsening.
questions.additionalInfo: The provider asks the nurse to identify which findings point to deterioration that needs faster action.

options.text: Supports worsening acute kidney injury
Less urgent monitoring picture

matrixRows.label: Rising creatinine with falling urine output
Stable creatinine with adequate hourly urine output
Improved intake with steady daily weight

questions.rationale: Acute kidney injury becomes more urgent when urine output falls, creatinine rises, and electrolyte complications emerge. Stable output and renal trends belong on the less urgent side because they do not signal deterioration.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of renal protection. The stem cue "Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Less urgent monitoring picture" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.

questions.takeaway: Falling urine plus rising creatinine is the shortcut to worsening AKI.
questions.speedCue: Creatinine up and urine down means kidney injury is moving the wrong way.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: "Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing" fits the safer response to consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections.
distractorRationales.d: "Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

visualRationale.title: central_line_infection_prevention
visualRationale.caption: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
visualRationale.conclusion: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
